title: Cold-water indigo vat
subtitle: Setting up and maintaining a fermentation indigo vat for dyeing wool and silk without heat
excerpt: A fermentation indigo vat uses iron, lime, and organic material (madder, bran, or ripe fruit) to chemically reduce indigo powder to its soluble form without synthetic reduction agents. Once established, the vat can be maintained and refreshed for weeks. The cold vat is slower to set up than a chemical vat but produces colours with more depth and variation.
sourceNotes: Fermentation indigo vat method follows Michel Garcia's fruit vat and historical European fermentation vat techniques as documented in Catharine Ellis and Joy Boutrup, The Art and Science of Natural Dyes (2019).

glossaryTerms.definition: The chemical process of removing oxygen from indigotin molecules to convert them from their insoluble blue form to soluble leuco-indigo (yellow-green). In a fermentation vat, this is achieved by microorganisms consuming oxygen in the vat liquid. In a chemical vat, sodium hydrosulphite (a reducing agent) is used instead. Reduction is temporary: leuco-indigo reverts to insoluble blue indigotin on exposure to air.

The regular feeding and care required to keep a fermentation indigo vat in working condition. A healthy vat needs warmth (above 20°C), correct pH (9-11), and periodic feeding with organic matter (bran, fruit, or madder) to maintain the microbial activity that keeps the indigo reduced. Signs of a healthy vat: yellow-green surface colour, purple-blue sheen, and a clean earthy smell.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Lime (calcium hydroxide) is caustic. Wear gloves and eye protection. Wash immediately if skin contact occurs.

content.text: Indigo does not dissolve in water in its blue form. It must be chemically reduced before it can bond to fibre.
in a fermentation vat uses bacteria that remove oxygen from the liquid, creating the conditions the indigo needs to dissolve.
means checking pH and feeding the bacteria regularly. The ADVANCED rating reflects the monitoring work, not the dyeing step itself.

items.name: Ripe banana, overripe fruit, or wheat bran (vat food)
Stainless steel or enamel pot or bucket

content.text: Slake the lime in a small amount of cold water to a paste. In the vat container, mix the indigo powder to a paste with cold water. Add the lime paste, the ferrous sulphate dissolved in a little warm water, and the fruit or bran. Add the 5 litres of warm water (around 30°C). Stir gently from the bottom to combine without introducing air bubbles.

Cover the vat and keep it in a warm place (above 20°C, ideally 25-30°C). Check after 24 hours: the vat surface should show a purple-bronze sheen and the liquid beneath should be yellow-green. Check the pH; it should be between 9 and 11. If the pH is below 9, add a small amount of lime paste. If the surface is still blue-purple with no yellow-green beneath, leave for another 24 hours.

Gently lower wet, scoured fibre into the vat without breaking the surface more than necessary. Hold the fibre below the surface for 5-15 minutes. Remove slowly and expose to air for 5-10 minutes; the fibre will turn from yellow-green to blue as the leuco-indigo oxidises. Dip again for more depth. Rinse in clean water after the final oxidation.
Step 4: Feed and maintain the vat
After each dyeing session, feed the vat with a small amount of fruit or bran and check the pH. Keep the vat covered and warm between uses. A well-maintained vat can remain active for several weeks.

items.problem: The vat stays blue and never turns yellow-green
items.cause: Reduction has not occurred: too much oxygen, pH too low, or vat too cold
items.fix: Check pH and add lime if below 9. Move the vat to a warmer location. Add more organic matter and wait another 24-48 hours. Stir very gently from the bottom only once.

items.problem: The fibre dips turn a dirty grey-green rather than clear blue
items.cause: Fibre entered before the vat was fully reduced, or too much iron
items.fix: Wait until the vat shows a clear yellow-green colour and purple sheen before dyeing. Excess iron produces greyed, dull results; reduce the ferrous sulphate in future batches.
